verses.fhb_text: Do not let your heart be troubled. You believe in the Father; believe also in me.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Jesus opens the Farewell Discourse with the call to faith in the Father alongside himself.

Renderings:
• "the Father" — Greek τὸν θεόν (Theos) distinguished from Jesus by the paired "believe also in me"; resolves to the Father per FHB-1.2-1 NT X-of-God general clause
• "the Father" rather than "our Father" — Jesus is the speaker; per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement, narrator-default "our Father" does not apply for Jesus's voice

Greek: ton theon (the God) → "the Father"

verses.fhb_text: In my Father's house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you?
verses.decision_notes: Jesus speaking with the possessive τοῦ πατρός μου — preserved as "my Father's house" per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement.

verses.fhb_text: And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again and take you to myself, so that where I am you may be also.
verses.decision_notes: Continuation of v.2's promise; Jesus speaking in first person.

verses.fhb_text: And you know the way to where I am going.
verses.decision_notes: Narrative bridge to Thomas's question in v.5.

verses.fhb_text: Thomas said to him, "Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?"
verses.decision_notes: "Lord" preserved as Thomas's vocative address per FHB-1.2-1 dialogue preservation. "Said" rather than "answered" — no prior question precedes this address.

verses.fhb_text: Jesus said to him, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Jesus's signature predicate "I am" claim — the way to the Father.

Renderings:
• "I am the way, and the truth, and the life" — predicate ἐγώ εἰμι with metaphor → normal lowercase per FHB-1.2-6 (this is not the absolute I AM of John 8:58)
• "the Father" — Greek τὸν πατέρα; Jesus speaking universal doctrine of access; preserved as he says it

Greek: egō eimi hē hodos kai hē alētheia kai hē zōē (I am the way and the truth and the life) → "I am the way, and the truth, and the life"

verses.fhb_text: If you had known me, you would have known my Father also. From now on you do know him and have seen him.
verses.decision_notes: "my Father" preserved per Greek τὸν πατέρα μου (possessive) per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement.

verses.fhb_text: Philip said to him, "Lord, show us the Father, and it is enough for us."
verses.decision_notes: "Lord" preserved as Philip's vocative address; "the Father" preserved as Philip's reference (no possessive in Greek).

verses.fhb_text: Jesus said to him, "Have I been with you so long, and you still do not know me, Philip? Whoever has seen me has seen the Father. How can you say, 'Show us the Father'?"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The hermeneutical key — to see Jesus is to see the Father.

Renderings:
• "the Father" preserved throughout — Jesus's universal teaching about who he reveals; no possessive in Greek

Greek: ho heōrakōs eme heōraken ton patera (the one having seen me has seen the Father) → "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father"

verses.fhb_text: Do you not believe that I am in the Father and the Father is in me? The words that I say to you I do not speak on my own authority, but the Father who dwells in me does his works.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father" preserved throughout — Jesus's mutual-indwelling teaching with no possessive in Greek.

verses.fhb_text: Believe me that I am in the Father and the Father is in me, or else believe on account of the works themselves.
verses.decision_notes: Restatement of v.10's mutual indwelling — appeal to the works as evidence.

verses.fhb_text: Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever believes in me will also do the works that I do; and greater works than these will he do, because I am going to the Father.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father" preserved per Greek τὸν πατέρα — Jesus's destination, no possessive in this clause.

verses.fhb_text: Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father" / "the Son" preserved — universal Father-Son glory teaching; no possessives in Greek.

verses.fhb_text: If you ask me anything in my name, I will do it.
verses.decision_notes: Restatement of v.13's promise in personal terms.

verses.fhb_text: If you love me, you will keep my commandments.
verses.decision_notes: Bridges to the Helper promise in v.16.

verses.fhb_text: And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper, the Holy Spirit, to be with you forever,
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First appearance of the Helper — apposition with the Holy Spirit established at v.16, matching scripture's own apposition at v.26.

Renderings:
• "another Helper, the Holy Spirit" — apposition pattern per FHB-1.2-7 F; scripture supplies "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" at v.26, FHB carries the name forward to the first promise
• "the Father" — Jesus's reference to the Father who will send the Helper; no possessive in Greek

Greek: allon paraklēton (another Paraclete / Helper) → "another Helper, the Holy Spirit"
verses.footnote: Jesus names this Helper as the Holy Spirit at verse 26 — FHB carries the name forward so readers meet the Spirit by name from the first promise.

verses.fhb_text: even the Spirit of truth, whom the world cannot receive, because it neither sees him nor knows him. You know him, for he dwells with you and will be in you.
verses.decision_notes: "Spirit of truth" preserved as Jesus's chosen title per FHB-1.2-7. Masculine pronouns for the Holy Spirit per FHB-7.3.6-1; lowercase per FHB convention.

verses.fhb_text: I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father-heart center of the chapter — Jesus will not leave the disciples fatherless.

Renderings:
• "orphans" preserved literally — Greek ὀρφανούς; FHB preserves the family-relational language that names the heart of Jesus's mission

Greek: orphanous (orphans) → "orphans"
verses.footnote: Jesus came to bring orphans home — this verse names the heart of why he came.

verses.fhb_text: Yet a little while longer and the world will see me no more, but you will see me. Because I live, you also will live.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus's resurrection promise to the disciples.

verses.fhb_text: In that day you will know that I am in my Father, and you in me, and I in you.
verses.decision_notes: "my Father" preserved per Greek τῷ πατρί μου (possessive). The mutual indwelling of v.10-11 extends to include disciples.

verses.fhb_text: Whoever has my commandments and keeps them, that is the one who loves me. And the one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and manifest myself to him.
verses.decision_notes: "my Father" preserved per Greek τοῦ πατρός μου (possessive).

verses.fhb_text: Judas (not Iscariot) said to him, "Lord, how is it that you will manifest yourself to us, and not to the world?"
verses.decision_notes: "Lord" preserved as Judas's vocative; parenthetical "not Iscariot" preserves John's clarification.

verses.fhb_text: Jesus replied, "If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our home with him."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father and the Son together make their home in those who love Jesus — the mirror of v.2.

Renderings:
• "my Father" preserved per Greek ὁ πατήρ μου (possessive)
• "replied" per FHB-9.14-1 — responding to Judas's question; less weighty than "answered" which is reserved for emphasis on the answering act
• "make our home with him" — Greek μονὴν παρ' αὐτῷ ποιησόμεθα; same noun (monē, "dwelling") as the "rooms" of v.2; the Father and Son moving INTO the believer is the inverse of v.2's prepared rooms

Greek: monēn par' autō poiēsometha (we will make a dwelling with him) → "make our home with him"
verses.footnote: The Father who prepares rooms in his house for us in verse 2 makes his home in those who love Jesus — both directions of intimacy in one chapter.

verses.fhb_text: Whoever does not love me does not keep my words. And the word that you hear is not mine but the Father's who sent me.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father's" preserved per Greek τοῦ πατρός (no possessive); Jesus naming the Father who sent him.

verses.fhb_text: These things I have spoken to you while still being with you.
verses.decision_notes: Transition to the Helper teaching in v.26.

verses.fhb_text: But the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name — he will teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Scripture's own apposition — "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" — preserved verbatim per FHB-1.2-7 F.

Renderings:
• "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" — scripture's own apposition (Greek ὁ παράκλητος, τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον); preserved verbatim
• em dash before "he will teach" — the Greek ἐκεῖνος ("that one") is a demonstrative resuming the subject after a long relative clause; em dash earns its place per FHB-9.8-1 (appositional emphasis that reframes the antecedent)

Greek: ho paraklētos, to pneuma to hagion (the Helper, the Holy Spirit) → "the Helper, the Holy Spirit"

verses.fhb_text: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid.
verses.decision_notes: Jesus's parting gift of peace; bookends the chapter (cf. v.1 "Do not let your heart be troubled").

verses.fhb_text: You heard me say to you, 'I am going away, and I will come to you.' If you loved me, you would have rejoiced, because I am going to the Father, for the Father is greater than I.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father is greater than I" preserved per Greek ὁ πατὴρ μείζων μού ἐστιν — Jesus's own statement of the Father's primacy in the Son's incarnate mission.

verses.fhb_text: And now I have told you before it takes place, so that when it does take place you may believe.
verses.decision_notes: Prophetic-fulfillment bridge.

verses.fhb_text: I will no longer talk much with you, for the ruler of this world is coming. He has no claim on me,
verses.decision_notes: "the ruler of this world" preserved per Greek ὁ τοῦ κόσμου ἄρχων — Satan as the contested authority Jesus is about to defeat at the cross.

verses.fhb_text: but I do as the Father has commanded me, so that the world may know that I love the Father. Rise, let us go from here.
verses.decision_notes: "the Father" preserved per Greek ὁ πατήρ (no possessive); Jesus's love for the Father shown through obedience.

chapter_notes: John 14 is part of Jesus's Farewell Discourse (John 13-17), spoken the night before the cross. The chapter centers on the Father-Son-Spirit relationship: Jesus going to the Father (vv.2-4, 12, 28), the way to the Father (v.6), the mutual indwelling of Father and Son (vv.10-11), the Father sending the Helper (vv.16, 26), and the Father and Son making home in those who love Jesus (v.23). Per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 grammatical agreement: Jesus speaks in first person to disciples; references to the Father preserve the Greek's distinction between τοῦ πατρός μου ("my Father" — possessive, vv.2, 7, 20, 21, 23) and τὸν πατέρα ("the Father" — universal teaching, vv.6, 8-13, 16, 24, 26, 28, 31). The narrator-default "our Father" does not apply when Jesus is the speaker. v.1 Theos resolves to "the Father" per NT X-of-God general clause. v.6 "I am the way, and the truth, and the life" is predicate egō eimi → normal lowercase per FHB-1.2-6. v.16 establishes "the Helper, the Holy Spirit" apposition per FHB-1.2-7 F (scripture supplies the apposition at v.26; FHB carries forward to first promise). v.23 "make our home with him" uses the same Greek noun (monē) as "many rooms" in v.2 — the Father's house has rooms for us, and the Father and Son come to make rooms in us.
